metadata.title: Online Conspiracy Groups: Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter

abstract: Soon after the 9/11 terrorist attacks, a number of reports traced the attacks to a UFO that had appeared over the World Trade Center Towers, to the machinations of a secret society called the Illuminati, to George Bush, President of the United States, and to Tony Blair, Prime Minister of the United Kingdom (Barkun 2003). Donald Trump was elected U.S. President despite propagating a range of conspiracy theories throughout his campaign, including allegations that climate change is a hoax perpetrated by the Chinese, that Barack Obama was not born in the United States, and that vaccines cause autism (Van Prooijen and Douglas 2018). Conspiracy theories are stories about "a powerful and secret group engaging in covert, malevolent, and organized activities against vulnerable groups" (DiFonzo 1125937A SRXXX10.

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
paragraphs: 2018:258). Given the emphasis on malevolent design, every conspiracy theory has three characteristics: (1) nothing happens by accident and everything is by design, (2) nothing is as it seems and innocence is deceptive, and (3) everything is connected (Barkun 2003:4).

Conspiracy theories are consequential, universal, emotional, and social (Van Prooijen and Douglas 2018). Atkinson, DeWitt, and Uscinski (2017:166) suggest Trump's electoral success hinged on mobilizing "an oftignored segment of the population" through conspiratorial discourse. Belief in COVID-19 conspiracy theories correlates with reduced social distancing, mask wearing, and vaccination rates (Bierwaczonek, Kunst, and Pich 2020;Pummerer et al. 2022). Half of Americans believe in at least one conspiracy theory, showing a glimpse of their universal nature (Oliver and Wood 2014). Conspiracy theories are common to diverse cultures, from theories of 5G signals transmitting COVID-19 that have spread in the United Kingdom and Latin America (Goodman and Carmichael 2020) to viewing modern technology as a Western plot to dominate villagers in rural Tanzania (West and Sanders 2003). Analysis of over 100,000 letters sent to major U.S. newspapers shows the presence of conspiracy theories has been stable for over 120 years (Uscinski and Parent 2014). Conspiracy theories arise from emotions of fear and anger and a lack of control (Whitson, Galinsky, and Kay 2015), and they are social because they entail intergroup beliefs that a powerful or hostile outgroup is conspiring against a perceiver's ingroup (Imhoff and Lamberty 2018). The significant increase in the use of social media platforms has led to online conspiracy groups where participants exchange interpretations and get attention from others (Grant et al. 2015).

Given the salience of conspiracy groups, any "study of a real society must include a study of conspiracy culture" (Bell and Bennion-Nixon 2001:147). Yet, there has been a remarkable neglect of online conspiracy groups and online conspiracy talk in sociology. For the most part, psychologists have studied the dispositions that lead to conspiratorial ideation and beliefs (e.g., Enders and Smallpage 2018;Oliver and Wood 2014), relying on crosssectional methods and eliciting conspiracy theory beliefs through surveys (e.g., Wood, Douglas, and Sutton 2012) or experiments that manipulate threat to the system (e.g., Whitson et al. 2015). This line of work has produced findings indicating that beliefs in conspiracy theories are correlated within individuals, even if the conspiracy theories are inconsistent, and has connected this finding to a conspiratorial mindset with a generalized predisposition of suspicion of all official authority (e.g., Wood et al. 2012). However, research on conspiratorial thinking seldom examines the social context in which conspiratorial talk flourishes.

A rapidly growing body of work studies the social environments that promote conspiracy talk. One line of work highlights the effects of algorithms on social structure; fake news gets more attention than truth and spreads faster on social media (Vosoughi, Roy, and Aral 2018), and individuals are sorted into echo chambers, thereby leading to polarization of opinions (Centola 2013;Törnberg 2018). Another body of work questions the primacy of algorithmic radicalization and directs attention to the social nature of online dynamics. For instance, echo chambers are not as pervasive as they were thought to be (Guess et al. 2018): "if online 'echo chambers' exist, they are a reality for relatively few people" (Guess 2021(Guess :1007)). In a similar vein, Ledwich and Zaitsev (2019) find that YouTube algorithms discourage people from visiting extremist sites, and Munger and Phillips (2022) indicate that far-right viewership on YouTube has declined since 2017, even more so when YouTube changed its algorithm in January 2018 to demote far-right sites. Bail (2021) reports evidence of false polarization, wherein social media users overestimate the degree of political differences with those who have opposing views, and leaderless demagoguery, wherein those who express extreme views with dramatic claims obtain status.

An emphasis on algorithmic determinism comes at the price of ignoring how individuals navigate and shape their discursive and social surroundings. Moving beyond recommendation algorithms requires attending to how and why individuals join and participate in online conspiracy talk. As DiFonzo (2018:257) notes, "conspiracy theorists didn't theorize their conspiracies. Conspiracy theories are heard and told; hearing and telling are things done with other people and in groups." Unlike surveys that have response bias, online social media allows for unobtrusive observation of public discourse (Vosoughi et al. 2018). Close study of conspiracy talk, using the methodology of natural language processing to extract statements propagating conspiracy theories from a larger corpus of text, is a powerful tool for understanding individual-level cognitions along with the social structure of interactions. What drives the adoption of new conspiracy theories? Do the theories proffered by participants aggregate into logics of action? Do individuals embrace consistent conspiracy logics of action, or do they subscribe to inconsistent ones? What is the role of automated accounts controlled by malign actors, known as bots, in online conspiracy talk (Ferrara et al. 2016)? A study of tweets and retweets that embody individual-level cognitions (conspiracy theories) can elucidate fundamental questions in the study of culture: whether conspiracy culture is unitary or more fragmented, whether individual schemas cumulate into logics of action, and whether and how individuals switch between them (DiMaggio 1997).

Given the dearth of evidence, our article's modest goal is to provide a descriptive baseline for the cognitive architecture and social propagation of conspiracy theories about COVID-19 on Twitter. Micro-blogging sites such as Twitter are discursive forums where people can exchange conspiratorial interpretations, but attention in the form of retweets is a scarce resource. A retweet is analogous to the propagation of a rumor: just as those who spread a rumor do not share their doubts about the rumor to a receiver (Heath, Bell, and Sternberg 2001), people propagate a conspiracy theory without their doubts. A retweet is also a form of conversation. The person retweeted will see the retweet as a response. The person retweeting can see the subsequent retweets by others and notice whether they repeat, agree, elaborate, or contradict the original message.

Focusing the investigation on COVID-19 conspiracy theories on Twitter allows us to track conspiracy theories over time from very early on, as opposed to other work on conspiracy theories that collected cross-sectional data after they became sufficiently established to be formalized as survey questions (e.g., Pummerer et al. 2022). After describing the patterns of conspiracy talk by human beings and bots, we inductively derive theoretical implications. Just as ethnographers use conversational data in organizations obtained through participant observation and develop a theoretical framework, we use computational linguistics techniques for description and then frame our theoretical arguments.

We use Twitter's public application programming interface (API) to collect approximately 700,000 COVID-19 tweets from 8,000 users dating from the start of the COVID-19 pandemic until July 2020. We identify conspiracy theories in users' tweets with unsupervised topic models. To surmount the historically poor performance of topic models such as Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) on Twitter data, we use the biterm topic model (BTM), a permutation of LDA specifically developed for short text, like tweets (Chen et al. 2015;Yan et al. 2013). We obtain a 150-topic model with 13 distinct COVID-19 conspiracy theory topics. These conspiracy theories aggregate into two logics of action: talk claiming the virus was a hoax or exaggerated threat (e.g., testing gives false positives, or hospitals are secretly empty) and talk that described it as a bioweapon spread on purpose (e.g., by Bill Gates, the Chinese, or a worldcontrolling cabal) (Uscinski et al. 2020). We distinguish between human and bot accounts using Botometer, a widely used Twitter bot classification algorithm (Davis et al. 2016).

We find that users first propagate gateway conspiracy theories-less extreme and more plausible conspiracy theories-before progressing to more extreme ones. In our data, a user's first conspiracy theory is a gateway 40 percent of the time, as opposed to 16 percent if they had picked conspiracy theories randomly. Event-history analyses show that individuals propagate new conspiracy theories and inconsistent conspiracy logics when they face a rising exogenous threat (proxied by the COVID-19 case rate) and when they receive attention in the form of retweets that signal peer approval. In sharp contrast, bots propagate conspiracy theories that proclaim COVID-19 to be a deliberately designed bioweapon, consistent with a goal of creating a moral panic. Bots are less responsive to the threat of a rising case rate and are less likely to enter via gateway conspiracies. If human beings are defensive, bots rely on offense.

These findings about individuals are consistent with a culture-as-toolkit perspective (Swidler 1986), which proposes that people use cultural resources (be they myths, explanations, or theories) to make sense of the world and construct practical strategies of action to cope with it. Participants in online conspiracy talk use preexisting cultural tools to cope with uncertainty and make sense of a threatening situation, and they propagate multiple coronavirus conspiracy theories, even incompatible versions, to lower the perceived threat.

Our study adds a social dimension to psychological research that shows belief in one conspiracy theory predicts belief in another (Swami et al. 2011). Wood and colleagues (2012) correlated Likert scales of the credibility of beliefs in different conspiracy theories about Princess Diana's and Osama bin Laden's deaths and found that individuals subscribed to multiple and inconsistent explanations of the deaths. Our research differs from prior work with our social focus by examining public broadcasting of private beliefs and interactions among participants. We find that participants learn what is valued by the community via the number of retweets a tweet receives, and attention in the form of retweets triggers propagation of new and contradictory conspiracy theories. Thus, propagation of multiple and inconsistent conspiracy theories is driven by rewards in the form of retweets. The longitudinal nature of our study lets us observe the sequence of individuals first adopting a gateway conspiracy theory, one that is plausible and easy to believe, followed by an increased likelihood of adopting more extreme ones. This temporal sequence and the propagation of inconsistent conspiracy logics is related to an increase in perceived uncertainty-the rising COVID-19 case rate.

Our article differs from current work on conspiracy theories in two fundamental respects. First, we complement accounts of echo chambers or algorithmic determinism by documenting how and when individuals enter and participate in online conspiracy talk. Echo chambers driven by recommendation algorithms may facilitate conspiracy theorizing, but our study suggests these communities emerge from the fundamentally social process of collective sensemaking in response to threats. Our finding that individuals tweet more conspiracies after receiving positive peer feedback in the form of retweets supports this interpretation. Second, psychological research, which finds that beliefs in one conspiracy theory predict the adoption of another theory, illuminates a conspiratorial mindset, wherein there is generalized predisposition of suspicion of all official authority. Work like Wood and colleagues (2012) relies on Adorno's (1950:92) explanation that "nuclear ideas tend to 'pull in' numerous other opinions and attitudes and thus, form a broad ideological system." By contrast, our findings support Bauman's (2005) and Eco's (2017) arguments that there is a collapse of ideologies in contemporary society and the only grand narrative, if any, is uncertainty, which produces a patchwork quilt of beliefs. We suggest that, by simultaneously subscribing to inconsistent theories, individuals engage in explanatory diversification: diversity is a hedge against increasing uncertainty.

sections.header_norm: mapping the cognitive structure of online conspiracy groups
paragraphs: In the early 1950s, there were two contrasting views of conspiracy theories in sociology. Adorno (1950) described conspiracy talk as irrational and located it in the authoritarian personality, whereas Lowenthal and Guterman (1949:8) described "conspiracy theorizing [as] a meaning-making cultural practice that was worth analyzing and studying." Following Adorno (1950) and Hofstadter (1964), most subsequent research investigated the individual cognitive factors that drive conspiratorial beliefs (see Douglas et al. 2019;Uscinski 2018). Conspiratorial beliefs are stronger among individuals with paranoid personalities (Darwin, Neave, and Holmes 2011) and are associated with belief in supernatural phenomena (Oliver and Wood 2014;Van Prooijen, Douglas, and De Inocencio 2018) and low feelings of social control (Uscinski and Parent 2014). Sunstein and Vermeule (2009) trace conspiracy beliefs to information processing deficiencies, Elster (1998) discusses communication problems, Osmundsen and colleagues (2021) suggest political motives are prominent, and Uscinski and Parent (2014) argue that "conspiracy theories are for losers" of elections or economies.

Lately, there has been a critique of the pathologization of conspiracy theories (Knight and Butter 2018), and revival of interest in conspiracy talk as a form of meaningmaking under uncertainty. Douglas, Sutton, and Cichocka (2017) suggest people are drawn to conspiracy theories to satisfy three motives: existential (e.g., the desire for control and security in the face of threats), epistemic (e.g., the desire for understanding and subjective certainty), and social (e.g., the desire to maintain a positive image of the self or group). The three motives may be interdependent, as a traumatic event can trigger existential motives, followed by epistemic search, and then social discourse (Van Prooijen and Douglas 2018). These explanations are dispositional accounts that emphasize individual motivations to believe in conspiracy theories, but they say little about online conspiracy groups and online conspiracy talk.

To understand online conspiracy groups and talk, we must build on the premise that conspiracy theories are a cultural element of sensemaking for individuals who are motivated to reject information from authoritative institutions like the state, the press, or universities, and instead become committed to alternative knowledge (Barkun 2003:27-28). Sensemaking, according to Weick (1995:4), is the process by which people "structure the unknown." Weick (1995:49) also observed that "sensemaking tends to be swift, which means we are more likely to see products than process." Thus, a sensemaking view of conspiracy talk requires examining the cues that individuals extract from the environment, the connections they make among cues, and using a frame to tie them together to make sense.

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
sections.header_raw: Threat and the Adoption of New Theories
sections.header_norm: threat and the adoption of new theories
paragraphs: Mortality salience, or fear of death, triggers terror and induces people to rely on proximal defense (e.g., my grandparents lived long, and I too can) or distal defenses that draw on cultural world views (Solomon, Greenberg, and Pyszczynski 2015). The COVID-19 pandemic has increased fear about one's well-being (Jungmann and Witthöft 2020). Additionally, anxiety-buffering distal defenses, such as relationships at work or with friends, have been undermined by job losses and lockdowns (Pyszczynski et al. 2021). As a result, individuals have to rely on world views to defend themselves. Some studies indicate a partisan divide; liberals view the virus as dangerous but have confidence in science and government (Bird and Ritter 2020), whereas conservatives tend to view the virus as less dangerous and assign blame via conspiracy theories (Romano 2020).

Schaller and colleagues (Schaller 2011;Schaller and Murray 2011;Schaller and Neuberg 2012) theorized that contextually aroused feelings of vulnerability to disease activate a behavioral immune system, that is, a suite of mechanisms ranging from prejudice against foreigners to conspiratorial theories about outsiders. Conspiracy theories have appeal because they provide congruent knowledge about the world; the epistemic content resolves gaps in official accounts and embodies novel insights (Douglas et al. 2017). In this sense, conspiracy theories are the consequence of the sensemaking process initiated to resolve ambiguity. Because threats may trigger conspiracy talk, we propose that people will adopt new conspiracy theories more as the threat from the COVID-19 pandemic increases: a simple proxy is the rising case rate.

If conspiracy theories are a means to insulate oneself from threats (death in the extreme case of COVID-19), the question is whether different conspiracy theories cohere together into an ensemble. Indeed, Sperber (1996) argues that the extent to which any new idea is considered plausible depends on related prior beliefs and discourses. Kashima (2000) found that stories become more stereotype-consistent as they are retold, consistent with Bartlett's (1932) idea that serial reproduction of a story leads to its conventionalization. These arguments suggest individual conspiracy theories cohere to form logics of action, that is, the cognitive and normative understandings people bring to an exchange relationship (DiMaggio 1994;Friedland and Alford 1991). For COVID-19, the range of conspiracy theories cumulated into two logics of action: it was an exaggerated hoax, and it was a bioweapon spread on purpose (Usinski et al. 2020).

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
paragraphs: Sharing and interpreting conspiracy theories increases preparedness for understanding a situation and leads to interpretive control (Fine and Turner 2001;Walker 1996). If conspiracy talk is a way of reducing anxiety and taming uncertainty, is there a sequence of talk wherein individuals enter through a gateway of plausible conspiracy talk and then transition to more extreme conspiracy theories?

As Weick (1995:61) notes, "Sensemaking is about plausibility, coherence, and reasonableness. Sensemaking is about accounts that are socially acceptable and credible." In turn, plausibility is "a feeling that something makes sense, feels right, is somehow sensible, (and) fits with what you know" (Helms and Mills 2017:53).

Since Kandel (1975) proposed that marijuana predisposed individuals to consume other more harmful substances and was a gateway into addiction, there has been considerable support for the gateway hypothesis (e.g., Mosher and Akins 2014). Similarly, Uscinski and Parent (2014) suggest individuals enter the world of conspiracy theories by subscribing to easily plausible theories. We extend their reasoning and propose that people begin with plausible conspiracy theories before progressing to more extreme ones.

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
paragraphs: Do individuals choose one logic over another? One view is that individuals prize cognitive consistency; this idea rests on a model of an individual as a reflective thinker who gathers and evaluates evidence (Pennycook and Rand 2021). Festinger (1957) described cognitive consistency as a basic need, like hunger, and developed the idea of cognitive dissonance, wherein individuals feel anxious about inconsistency and deal with it by ignoring conflicting information or adding cognitions to reconcile the effect. In a related vein, Heider (1958) proposed that individuals balance their evaluations such that the product of all triadic evaluations is positive. More recent work suggests consistency serves a basic epistemic signal: inconsistency implies an error in one's system of beliefs (Gawronski 2012). Kruglanski and Shteynberg (2012) argue that cognitive consistency is desired because it validates a system of beliefs. Thus, inconsistency indicates a disconfirmed hypothesis that demands revision.

However, the model of a reflective thinker who pays attention to empirical disconfirmation is difficult to generalize to participants in conspiracy talk in online forums. One reason is that "no matter how much evidence their adherents accumulate, belief in a conspiracy theory ultimately becomes a matter of faith rather than proof. . . . In the end, the theory becomes nonfalsifiable, because every attempt at falsification is dismissed as a ruse" (Barkun 2003:7). A second reason is that individuals in online conspiracy groups are seeking to make sense of a threat based on their intuition (Pennycook and Rand 2021). In so doing, they are like bricoleurs, improvisers who piece together different elements available at hand to solve the problem (Levi-Strauss 1996:16-17). In this line of reasoning, each conspiracy theory is a tool in a toolkit of ideas, explanations, and myths. More generally, culture is a collage of "diverse, often, conflicting symbols, stories, and guides to action" (Swidler 1986:277). Research demonstrates that people cannot put together coherent accounts of their choices and instead rely on myths, lay theories, common sense ideas and adages, and other cultural resources that they add up and mix together (e.g., Lizardo and Strand 2010;Patterson 2014;Small, Harding, and Lamont 2010;Swidler 2003).

Thus, culture is not a "switchman directing an engine propelled by interests," but instead a toolkit that allows people to make sense of and cope with the world (Swidler 1986:277). Swidler (1986:278-79) distinguishes between settled times and unsettled times and observes that in unsettled times "people formulate, flesh out new lines of action." Supporting this notion, new conspiracy theories flourish following major societal events and upheaval, as individuals develop new ways to interpret and make sense of these changing situations (Uscinski and Parent 2014). Swidler (2003:30) uses an evocative metaphor of echolocation and notes that "bats know where they are by bouncing sounds off the objects around them. Similarly, people orient themselves partly by bouncing their ideas off the cultural alternatives made apparent in their environments." In so doing, individuals are likely to be flexible and use different explanations. Because any specific conspiracy theory may invite opposition in social exchanges or even falsification, actors who are flexible in their choice of theories are better equipped to maintain their defense against reality. If conspiracy theories enable people to make sense of the world, we should observe that they are flexible enough to propagate inconsistent conspiracy theories over time, or even propagate contradictory conspiracy theories simultaneously. We propose that people are inconsistent in their conspiracy theory propagation and that inconsistency rises in response to threat.

One view is that individuals prize cognitive consistency; this idea rests on a model of an individual as a reflective thinker who gathers and evaluates evidence (Pennycook and Rand 2021). Festinger (1957) described cognitive consistency as a basic need, like hunger, and developed the idea of cognitive dissonance, wherein individuals feel anxious about inconsistency and deal with it by ignoring conflicting information or adding cognitions to reconcile the effect.
sections.preview_tail: Because any specific conspiracy theory may invite opposition in social exchanges or even falsification, actors who are flexible in their choice of theories are better equipped to maintain their defense against reality. If conspiracy theories enable people to make sense of the world, we should observe that they are flexible enough to propagate inconsistent conspiracy theories over time, or even propagate contradictory conspiracy theories simultaneously. We propose that people are inconsistent in their conspiracy theory propagation and that inconsistency rises in response to threat.

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
sections.header_raw: Attention and Inconsistency as a Norm
sections.header_norm: attention and inconsistency as a norm
paragraphs: In online conspiracy talk, some claims and individuals get attention and others may not. In turn, as Hilgartner and Bosk (1988) point out, attention is a scarce resource, and only individuals whose claims are novel, dramatic, and showcase negative emotions get attention. Social media has magnified these dynamics. Vosoughi andcolleagues (2018:1146) report that false news spreads significantly "further, faster, and more broadly than the truth," because it is both more novel and induces fear and disgust among its readers. An important feedback mechanism in online social media like Twitter is the number of shares (retweets) a user's post (tweet) receives. Tweeting is a form of echolocation; users broadcast their thoughts into the digital ether and sense the communities' beliefs by observing what bounces back. When users receive hundreds or even thousands of retweets, they discover that the content of their tweet is highly valued by the community.

Internal consistency is viewed positively and is even seen as a basic psychological need (Gawronski and Brannon 2019), but peer approval is also important. Losch andCacioppo (1990) extend Festinger's (1957) findings by reporting that inconsistency-reducing behaviors only occur when inconsistency-related arousal is perceived negatively, not when it is perceived positively. For people considering how to make sense of the COVID-19 pandemic and what accounts to propagate, when they see others propagate inconsistent theories and find that attention goes to unexpected, unambiguous, and confrontational claims, inconsistency is likely to become a norm. Indeed, sharing of unauthorized knowledge in the form of a conspiracy theory is a signal of identity and group belonging. Attention is a double-edged sword in online conspiracy talk. On one side, the greater the attention individuals receive, the more likely they are entrenched in existing conspiracy theories, as peer approval means they have selected the "correct" explanation for the COVID-19 pandemic. On the other side, attention may drive individuals to adopt new and novel conspiracy theories. Here, peer approval concerns the broader project of reality denial, rather than the specific conspiracy theory, and it may rouse individuals to combine inconsistent logics because it is dramatic, unexpected, and confrontational.

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
paragraphs: Our discussion so far has centered on how human beings engage in conspiracy talk to cope with the pandemic threat. Yet social media is also populated by bots that pose as human actors and seek to sow societal distrust, discord, and division. The social discourse is affected by bots, in part, because human users propagate (in Twitter, retweet) bot statements just as much as human user statements (Shao et al. 2018). The role of bots in coronavirus conspiracy talk has already been noted as an important topic of research (Himelein-Wachowiak et al. 2021). We propose there are detectable differences between human actor and bot conspiracy theory propagation. Because bots do not engage in sensemaking, we anticipate bots are (1) less likely to enter via gateway conspiracies, (2) less responsive to rising case counts, and (3) more likely to propagate bioweapon conspiracies over hoax conspiracies.

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
paragraphs: We study conspiracy theories surrounding the pandemic caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus (also called COVID-19 or the coronavirus). As of August 2022, there have been 600 million COVID-19 infections and over 6.4 million deaths globally. Just as the virus has proliferated and mutated, so too have conspiracy theories regarding its existence, origin, spread, and impact. Coronavirus conspiracy theories have significant acceptance among the U.S. public (see Uscinski et al. 2020), and belief in them correlates with lower approval of social distancing and vaccination against the virus (Bierwiaczonek et al. 2020;Pummerer et al. 2022). Coronavirus conspiracy theories offer unique insight because we observe the growth and evolution of an online community and its conspiracy theories from inception.

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
paragraphs: Our data on coronavirus conspiracies come from Twitter. We streamed the free Twitter application programming interface (API) for two common coronavirus conspiracy hashtags, #coronahoax and #virushoax, from February 17th to May 22nd, 2020, collecting all usernames and tweets containing these hashtags. Twitter limits the volume of recovered tweets per day, meaning if the query volume exceeds a designated threshold, researchers receive a pseudo-random sample of the query. As long as the number of tweets returned by the keyword search remains below the threshold (~600 tweets per minute), "all matching tweets are likely provided by Twitter" (Campan et al. 2018:3638). By streaming these two specific hashtags, as opposed to more general COVID-19 related terms, we stayed below the rate limit throughout our entire period of observation and collected the complete sample of Tweets using #coronahoax or #virushoax.

Of course, not every COVID-19 conspiracy tweet uses these hashtags. The purpose of the keyword search is to identify our population of interest. To build our corpus of conspiracy tweets, we collected the complete tweet history from the beginning of 2020 onward for each user who tweeted using #coronahoax or #virushoax. Next, we filtered the sample of tweets to those discussing COVID-19 using a dictionary of terms provided by Gallagher and colleagues (2021), resulting in a final sample of 8,000 users with 700,000 tweets and retweets. To simplify the analysis, we do not distinguish between original tweets, retweets, or quote tweets (where a user shares another person's tweet with a comment). We also collected account metadata, including the number of tweets, retweets, and followers for each account. This sampling strategy has the attendant limitations of publicly accessible Twitter data (see Part S1 of the online supplement).

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
paragraphs: Our limited goal is to identify when users propagate specific COVID-19 conspiracy theories on Twitter. We divided this methodological challenge into two stages. First, we identified and classified the diverse array of coronavirus conspiracies present in our data using topic modeling. Second, given this classification scheme, we followed an empirical protocol to identify when users propagate conspiracy theories. To circumvent the wellknown difficulties of tweet classification, we pooled information across a user's tweets. In both stages, we guided our quantitative approach with in-depth inductive readings of the tweets and topic model outputs. Doing so melds the richness of qualitative context analysis with the statistical rigor and power of modern natural language processing (NLP) techniques (Nelson 2020). We began this iterative process by randomly sampling several thousand virus-related tweets, reading them, and inductively developing a list of potential conspiracy theories present in the data.

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
paragraphs: A litany of conspiracy theories were present in our data, but demarking their boundaries and classifying them posed a significant challenge. It was rare that a single tweet would clearly articulate a conspiracy theory. Instead, many users made vague claims that, as the rumors literature tells us, are particularly effective (Allport and Postman 1947):

You and your sinister government will be held to account over your lies and deception. The truth on covid19 will be coming soon Propaganda only works if people believe it. All of you that believe this sensationlised propaganda filled project fear of sick ulterior motives spewed out by controlled media, politicians, doctors & elitists, its YOU that are holding our world to ransom. Its just a bad winter virus It is clear that these tweets sow distrust around COVID-19, but the content of this alternative worldview is opaque. Even when more detail is provided, users frequently veiled perpetrators' identities, allowing the imagination of their audience to fill in the blanks:

Because #agenda21 wich is being rolled out under the false flag #Plandemic #covidhoax #scamdemic #coronahoax and backed by the players involved in creating the #newworldorder ("they" dont like happy free people. "They" are trying to bring back a Roman style rulership).

Despite this general trend toward ambiguity, certain individuals (e.g., Bill Gates and Anthony Fauci) were frequent bogeymen, with a wide array of conspiracy claims circulating about them: Bill Gates advocated for mass vaccinations to sterilize the population, for mind control, to track them via geolocation, to profit, or to kill. Vaccines accomplish this goal by injecting microchips, poisoning, or altering DNA. Gates conspired with the Chinese government, the U.S. government, a "global cabal," Anthony Fauci, the World Health Organization, or he acted alone. These arrays of motives, mechanisms, and conspirators are inexhaustive, nonexclusive, fungible, and rarely co-present in a single tweet. Given the heterogeneity and ambiguity of claims, we found classifying in terms of genres or categories significantly more tractable than individual conspiracies. All the above combinations would be grouped under the umbrella of Bill Gates vaccine conspiracies.

Yet even with this higher-order classification, determining whether users were propagating a conspiracy theory, at the level of tweet, is unclear. Take the following tweet: "Lockdown opened eyes on: * all vaccines * 5G * Fauci giving $6 million to China to study coronaviruses * WHO, Bill Gates & criminal Big Pharma cabal * exposed enemies of humanity, freedom & their agenda * Autism & infant deaths drop due to NO VAC-CINES!" In a single tweet, the author alludes to five distinct conspiracy theories: 5G towers causing coronavirus, Fauci collaborating with China to create COVID-19 in a lab, Bill Gates' involvement, "their" (globalist) control, and non-COVID-19 vaccine conspiracies. Does merely mentioning 5G count as the propagation of the 5G conspiracy theory? Such a dilemma, as well as the ambiguous tweets presented previously, highlights the difficulty in identifying conspiracy propagation from an individual tweet. It seems likely that the author believes, to some extent, that 5G towers play a role in the pandemic, but we cannot say so with certainty. Rather than discard this information, we develop a methodology, presented below, to identify conspiracy propagation by pooling information across multiple tweets using topic models.

Such a dilemma, as well as the ambiguous tweets presented previously, highlights the difficulty in identifying conspiracy propagation from an individual tweet. It seems likely that the author believes, to some extent, that 5G towers play a role in the pandemic, but we cannot say so with certainty. Rather than discard this information, we develop a methodology, presented below, to identify conspiracy propagation by pooling information across multiple tweets using topic models.

paragraphs: Topic modeling is an unsupervised machinelearning technique commonly used to identify topics (i.e., clusters of related words) from textual data (Blei 2012). The model represents each document as a combination of latent topics that is "generated" by a probabilistic draw over the topics' sets of words. Sociologists have used topic models to study such diverse contexts as Afghan radical groups (Karell and Freedman 2019), the Federal Reserve (Fligstein, Brundage, and Schultz 2017), and the shifting logics of the women's liberation movement (Nelson 2021). Topic models have proven particularly fruitful in studying the spread of misinformation and fake news on social media platforms (Jamison et al. 2020;Walter, Ophir, and Jamieson 2020). For example, Smith and colleagues (2021) used topic models to study how influential actors spread disinformation narratives on Twitter during the 2017 French presidential elections.

Topic modeling offers several key advantages over manual-coding methods, such as qualitative content analysis (Hannigan et al. 2019). For one, it does not require the researcher to impose a dictionary or set of rules upon the text. As a result, these algorithms may identify patterns in the data that human coders may miss. More practically, manual coding is infeasible with large corpora. Not only do computational methods scale with ease when dealing with hundreds of thousands or millions of documents, their ability to discern subtle relationships in the data strengthens with the additional information.

Topic models rely on co-occurrence data and suffer from data sparsity problems when trained on short text (Hong and Davison 2010). As a result, topic models designed for paragraphs or pages of text, such as Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) or Structural Topic Models (STM) produce poor quality topics on Twitter. Instead, we use the biterm topic model (BTM), a permutation of LDA specifically developed for short text like Twitter (Chen et al. 2015;Yan et al. 2013). It overcomes the data sparsity problem by modeling the co-occurrence patterns of biterms (pairs of words) across the whole corpus.

Although powerful and scalable, topic models suffer from inaccuracy and researcher degrees of freedom (Chuang et al. 2013). The sensitivity to hyperparameter values means practitioners deploying these techniques using the default settings in software packages will produce low-quality models (DiMaggio 2015). One potential solution is to train dozens or even hundreds of models using different hyperparameter values and select the best-performing model. "Best" here is defined by performance on a quantitative metric such as coherence, that is, the degree of semantic similarity between high-scoring words in the topics (Röder, Both, and Hinneburg 2015). Such an approach has its merits, particularly for computer scientists seeking to maximize out-of-sample predictive power, but it has several limitations. Most pertinently, researchers have found performance on quantitative evaluations to be negatively correlated with human assessments of topic quality (Chang et al. 2009). Statistical performance does little to guarantee theoretical insight.

We instead follow the approach favored by social scientists, who emphasize the role of iterative and interpretative engagement with both the input text and the topic model output (Boyd-Graber, Mimno, and Newman 2014;Nelson 2020). Hannigan and colleagues (2019:614) depict topic modeling as a process of rendering, "to render meaning in this manner is to engender engagement with data, where the researcher zooms in and zooms out based on distant reading and representations of meaning structures." Nelson (2020) crystalizes this insight further, articulating a framework of computationally grounded theory, wherein researchers meld the rigorous theory-centric approaches of grounded theory with the scalability and reproducibility of computational methods. The union of "quality and quantity, breadth and depth, . . . close and distant" fortifies model quality and helps develop rich theoretical insight (Nelson 2020:5). Instead of maximizing quantitative performance, hyperparameters are chosen such that they maximize the interpretability and usefulness of the topic model to the author (DiMaggio 2015). As with quantitative metrics, researchers can rely on a variety of qualitative indicators of topic performance. Common practice includes examining the top words in each topic for semantic coherence (breadth) and reading the documents that rate highly in each topic (depth). A well-performing or high-quality topic (1) is one where the words have strong semantic similarity, (2) is differentiated from other topics, and (3) can be understood by the author.

Our objective is to create high-quality topics corresponding to categories of conspiracy theories (e.g., Bill Gates, 5G). These topics should be distinct from both non-conspiracy topics and one another. Note we are not concerned with the quality of non-conspiracy topics, and what maximizes the quality of conspiracy topics may produce worse nonconspiracy topics. As noted previously, we began our engagement with these data by reading thousands of tweets and developing a rough classification scheme. This in-depth reading provided a sense of which conspiracies were present in the data and the kind of topics we hoped to produce. With this guide, we began the iterative process of unsupervised training, manual evaluation, and hyperparameter tuning. Repeating this procedure many times resulted in a final model of 150 topics, α = 1, and β = .005. With 150 topics, we found our model did an excellent job discerning between conspiracy and non-conspiracy content. For example, it learned two distinct China topics; one captured non-conspiracy discourse (e.g., china, chinese, world, wuhan, country) and the other specifically identified the Chinese lab conspiracy (e.g., lab, fauci, wuhan, research, china). For more details on the fine-tuning of the topic model, see Part S2 of the online supplement. We evaluate the semantic coherence of the topic model in Part S4 of the supplement and find strong quantitative performance as well (see Table S2).

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
paragraphs: Having finished training the topic model, our next step is to formally determine which topics correspond to conspiracy theories. Despite our best efforts, the topics may not map neatly onto conspiracy theories. Even topics that well-identify a particular conspiracy theory might also capture non-conspiracy content. To avoid the false attribution of conspiracy theory propagation, we only label a topic a conspiracy theory if all tweets in the topic are clear instances of coronavirus conspiracy theories. Specifically, we require that the topic meets three criteria: (1) the top terms plausibly relate to a conspiracy theory, (2) prototypical tweets (i.e., tweets the model rates highly in that topic) clearly articulate a conspiracy theory, and (3) tweets with a moderate amount of the topic (less than 10 percent) also propagate the conspiracy theory. This third stage is essential because many topics that at first appear conspiratorial will contain a large amount of non-conspiracy theory content. Our inductive reading of tweets revealed several types of anti-mask conspiracy theories:

Absolutely. The mask mandate comes all the way from the top of the deep state command chain. It's part of pushing their agenda to demoralize and dehumanize us as they try to assume control of every aspect of our life in the name of "public safety" . . . it's a total calculated plan Evidence suggests viruses can enter the brain via olfactory nerves which connect directly with the area of the brain dealing with memory. By wearing a mask exhaled viruses cannot escape and concentrate in the olfactory nerves and travel into the brain Yet our topic models failed to disentangle conspiracy theory and non-conspiracy theory mask content:
If you're wearing a mask, you might as well be wearing a ball gag!! Wearing a mask to stop a virus is like putting up a chain-link fence to stop mosquitoes.

Through this three-step process, we identified 13 topics that can be unambiguously classified as conspiracy theories. The full list of these topics and their top keywords are given in Table S1 in the online supplement. Note that our methodology produces a conservative estimate for the diversity of conspiratorial beliefs on the platform. Because our model does not sufficiently identify anti-mask conspiracy theories, they are not represented in subsequent analyses. For more details on our reduction from 150 to 13 topics, see Part S3 of the supplement.

Table 1 presents the top words for selected conspiracy theory and non-conspiracy theory topics. For the non-conspiracy theory topics, we see clear and coherent topics around school closings, economic stimulus, and contact tracing. Likewise, our topic model produces coherent topics for conspiracy theories. The top words in the Bill Gates conspiracy theory, unsurprisingly, are "gate" (lemmatized from Gates), "bill," and "vaccine," along with words relating to common conspiracy theories surrounding Bill Gates. "Event" refers to Event 201, a 2019 simulation exercise hosted by the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation that simulated the effects and global response to a fictional coronavirus originating in pigs. Conspiracy theorists believe Event 201 is evidence that Bill Gates orchestrated the pandemic. A topic can, and frequently does, contain more than one unique conspiracy theory, and should be thought of as a genre of conspiracy theory in which several related versions exist. We refer to each of these 13 conspiracy categories as conspiracies for simplicity. We evaluated the semantic coherence of these 13 topics and report strong quantitative performance (see Part S4 and Table S2 in the online supplement) (see also Mimno et al. 2011;Röder et al. 2015).

In categorizing these conspiracy theories, we follow Uscinski and colleagues (2020), who distinguish between conspiracy theories that downplay the threat posed by the coronavirus (hoax) and conspiracy theories surrounding the origin and nefarious purposes of the virus (bioweapon). This distinction is not merely researcher imposed. Users noted this dichotomy as well: "The only questions that arise in sane minds are . . . Is this biological control war on populations by govts using lab altered viruses, or is it merely an exaggerated illusionary psyops using just an average flu."

Hoax conspiracies either deny the existence of the virus entirely or imply that the threat posed by the virus is greatly exaggerated. Conspiracies in this vein include the empty hospitals conspiracy, which postulates that hospitals are secretly empty (i.e., no COVID-19 patients), and the miscounted deaths conspiracy, which theorizes that doctors are counting routine deaths from heart failure or car crashes as COVID-19 fatalities to manipulate the death statistics. Bioweapon conspiracies acknowledge or exaggerate the severity of the threat but claim it is deliberately created or used by malicious actors to achieve nefarious ends. Examples include the China-created coronavirus conspiracy (the Chinese government developed and released the virus) and the Bill Gates topic. Table 2 lists the conspiracies we identified and whether we categorize the strain as a bioweapon or a hoax.

sections.preview_tail: Bioweapon conspiracies acknowledge or exaggerate the severity of the threat but claim it is deliberately created or used by malicious actors to achieve nefarious ends. Examples include the China-created coronavirus conspiracy (the Chinese government developed and released the virus) and the Bill Gates topic. Table 2 lists the conspiracies we identified and whether we categorize the strain as a bioweapon or a hoax.

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
paragraphs: Having developed and validated our topic model, the next step is to identify if and when users propagate a given conspiracy theory. Our in-depth reading of the tweets revealed that determining conspiracy endorsement from a single tweet, even with careful inspection by a human coder, is challenging, and automation only magnifies the difficulty. However, our interest is not in classifying tweets but in classifying users. With this shift in the unit of analysis in mind, we present a simple method to pool information across a user's tweets. By aggregating the content of users' tweets, we more accurately identify when they propagate different conspiracies.

We begin with a brief discussion of tweetlevel classification using topic models. For each user, u, we have their full tweet history of coronavirus-related tweets, d ∈ D. For each tweet, d, we estimate the distribution of topics contained in the tweet, θ d , using our trained topic model. Let θ dt denote the share of topic t in tweet d, where the sum of topic shares in a tweet equals one:

When a tweet almost entirely belongs to a single conspiracy topic (θ dt > .5), it is easy to say that the user propagates the conspiracy theory. In practice, it is rare for a tweet to be dominated by a single topic, and using a high threshold results in many false negatives, where we incorrectly determine the user did not propagate the conspiracy theory. Yet setting a lower threshold (e.g., θ dt > .1) risks a high degree of false positives. Thus, there is not enough information contained within a single tweet to accurately determine conspiracy theory propagation.

Note that our theoretical interest is in whether users, not tweets, propagate conspiracy theories. This subtle distinction points to a solution. By pooling information across the collection of tweets by a single user, we can decrease our false negative and false positive rates simultaneously (Alvarez-Melis and Saveski 2016; Hong and Davison 2010). One tweet may be a noisy signal, but aggregating the topic distribution across multiple tweets provides a clearer signal. To determine when users propagate conspiracy theories, we aggregate over all tweets in the prior week by summing their topic distributions. Let D iw denote all tweets by user i in week w, and θ iwt denote the aggregate topic share for topic t by user i in week w:

Without further adjustment, this approach would inadvertently consider highly active tweeters conspiracy theory propagators, not because they propagated the conspiracy, but because the topic comprises a minuscule share of many tweets. To correct for this, we floor the tweet-topic distribution to remove noise and only aggregate over tweets with a significant fraction of the conspiratorial topic. Formally, if θ dt < .1, we set θ dt = 0. Aggregating over this floored distribution, we say i propagates conspiracy t in w if θ iwt > .5. 1 With these definitions completed, we can now define the main dependent variables of our analyses.

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
paragraphs: New conspiracy count. This is a discrete count variable indicating the number of new conspiracy theories an individual propagates (tweets about) in the following week, which the same user has never before propagated.

Incompatible conspiracy theory. To measure conspiracy incompatibility, we follow Uscinski and colleagues (2020), who Neither distinguish between exaggerated threat and bioweapon conspiracies. We consider the hoax and bioweapon conspiracies to be incompatible, given that hoax conspiracies downplay the threat posed by the coronavirus, whereas bioweapon conspiracies acknowledge or exaggerate it. We define conspiracy incompatibility via an indicator variable that equals 1 if the individual propagates conspiracy theories in both the hoax and bioweapon categories.

We have two dependent variables of interest with respect to logical contradictions. Contradictory same week is an indicator variable that takes a value of 1 if a user endorses both a bioweapon conspiracy and a hoax conspiracy in the same week. Contradictory ever is an indicator variable that takes a value of 1 if a user endorses a conspiracy that contradicts any conspiracy they endorsed in the past. Thus, if user i endorses the false-positives conspiracy in week 8 and the Bill Gates conspiracy in week 12, then contradictory ever = 1 and contradictory same week = 0.

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
paragraphs: Threat. To measure the threat that could make individuals engage in conspiracy talk, we enter the one-week percentage change in the coronavirus infection case rate in the United States. We favor the U.S. increase over the focal-state increase because of the significant loss of observations that would be needed to match users to states based on their profile data. We use the U.S. case rate because U.S. case rates are widely reported across the world, and our sample predominately hails from the United States. We model threat as the percentage change in case count, rather than the count of cases, because sustained high case counts result in threat desensitization (Stevens, Oh, and Taylor 2021).

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
paragraphs: We measure peer attention as the maximum number of retweets a user received for any of their conspiratorial tweets, log transformed, as max retweets. We select the maximum, rather than the average, because the average number of retweets is extremely low and the maximum appropriates "going viral" (Goel, Watts, and Goldstein 2012).

Bot likelihood. Bots may differ from humans systematically in their conspiratorial endorsement. The challenge in discerning automated accounts (bots) from human actors on social media has received significant attention in both academic and public policy circles (Ferrara et al. 2016;Subrahmanian et al. 2016). A variety of methods exist to detect bots, including graph-based detection, crowdsourcing, and supervised machinelearning techniques, whereby researchers train a classifier on an annotated set of human and bot accounts (for a recent review, see Alothali et al. 2018). The most prominent machine-learning classifier is Botometer (formerly BotOrNot), jointly developed by the Observatory on Social Media and the Network Science Institute at Indiana University (Davis et al. 2016). To date, Botometer has been used in hundreds of scientific papers to help understand the role of bots in political polarization (Luceri et al. 2019), lowcredibility and fake news (Shao et al. 2018;Vosoughi et al. 2018), and social movement mobilization (Salge and Karahanna 2018).

Botometer interfaced with Twitter's API to extract over 1,000 account features. These features fall under six broad categories. Network features (e.g., degree, clustering coefficient, network centrality) are measured using retweets, mentions, and hashtags by the account. User features include the account's language, age, and geographic location. Temporal features capture the timing patterns of account behavior. Accounts that tweet at the same time every day or retweet at superhuman speed are likely to be flagged as bots.

Friend features are built using the account's social media contacts. Content features are constructed by analyzing the linguistic information contained in the tweet. Part-ofspeech tagging is particularly important here. Finally, the algorithm computes sentiment features, such as happiness, emoticons, and arousal-dominance-valence. Using these features, Botometer estimates the likelihood an account belongs to a bot through an ensemble of random forest classifiers. For a discussion of Botometer's limitations as they relate to our analyses, see Part S5 of the online supplement.

One final strength of Botometer is that the algorithm outputs the conditional probability that the account with its bot score or higher belongs to a bot. This value, unlike a strict 0 or 1 binary classifier, enables researchers to modify their tolerance for false positives/ negatives. We follow Davis and colleagues (2016) and create an indicator variable, bot, that takes a value of 1 if the account's Botometer threshold exceeds .4. Approximately one in every six accounts in our data is considered a bot with this cutpoint (see Figure S1 in the supplement). In additional analyses presented in Tables S3 and S4 in the online supplement, we vary the threshold at which we binarize the distribution and demonstrate robustness across a variety of different cutpoints.

Prior conspiracy count. Users may endorse a finite number of possible conspiracies; an account that has endorsed all 13 conspiracies cannot add any new ones. We thus control for the number of prior endorsed conspiracies through a linear and a quadratic term, prior conspiracy count and prior conspiracy count 2 , indicating the number of unique conspiracies the user has propagated.

Log degree. We control for the network position of the individual user in the conspiracy community. 2 There are many network statistics to choose from, but we select degree, the total number of ties a user has in our undirected network, as our primary control because it is easily interpreted and indicates how well-connected a user is in the network (Borgatti 2005). We find that degree correlates highly with other measures of network centrality, such as closeness centrality or betweenness centrality. We add one and log transform degree to reduce variable skew.

Average log tweet count. Because we measure conspiratorial endorsement by aggregating tweets, highly active users are disproportionately likely to be identified as propagating conspiracies. To adjust for this measurement bias, we include the average number of virus-related tweets users send each week, log transformed. We measure account activity at the user level, as opposed to the user-week level, because heightened account activity week-to-week may be a sign of arousal in response to perceived threat.

Week. The evolution of conspiracy theories over time, as well as their dissemination by prominent media figures both offline and on Twitter, may play an independent role in driving users to adopt conspiracy theories. Including week fixed effects, although the soundest empirical approach to handle temporal variation, is inappropriate because our independent variable of interest, COVID-19 case rate change, does not vary within week. Instead, we model the linear time trend by including a single variable, week, which indicates the week of the year.

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
paragraphs: We model our first dependent variable, new conspiracy count, using Poisson regression because it is a discrete count variable. Poisson regression models are a generalized linear model in which the dependent variable follows the Poisson distribution. We estimate conspiracy incompatibility via event history analyses (hazard rate analysis). In event history analysis, one estimates the expected duration until an event's occurrence. In our case, we estimate the duration until a user endorses an incompatible conspiracy theory. We estimate this using the Weibull specification of the hazard rate (r(t) = βk (β′x) k-1 ). The Weibull specification allows for smooth increase or decrease in the hazard rate over time (in our data, it decreased), and can also allow the effects of covariates to amplify or attenuate over time (in our data, such a specification was not needed).

For conspiracy contradiction weekly, we estimate multiple failure per individual models, because the same individual may experience contradictory endorsement for multiple weeks. For conspiracy contradiction ever, we estimate single failure per individual models because once an individual has endorsed contradictory conspiracies, their status is permanently flipped. We divide the data into user-week segments and lead the dependent variables by one week to avoid potential confounding effects between the independent variables and conspiratorial endorsement.

The Weibull specification allows for smooth increase or decrease in the hazard rate over time (in our data, it decreased), and can also allow the effects of covariates to amplify or attenuate over time (in our data, such a specification was not needed).

For conspiracy contradiction weekly, we estimate multiple failure per individual models, because the same individual may experience contradictory endorsement for multiple weeks. For conspiracy contradiction ever, we estimate single failure per individual models because once an individual has endorsed contradictory conspiracies, their status is permanently flipped. We divide the data into user-week segments and lead the dependent variables by one week to avoid potential confounding effects between the independent variables and conspiratorial endorsement.

paragraphs: Before estimating models, inspection of the data offers useful clues to what triggers individuals to propagate conspiracy theories. We begin by tracing the ebbs and flows of the different conspiracy theories over time. Figure figure 2. Pearson Correlations for Conspiratorial Endorsement within User Note: A positive correlation indicates that if a user endorses conspiracy A, they are also more likely to endorse conspiracy B. The final column, bot likelihood, indicates which conspiracy topics are more prominent among bots.

1a shows the number of tweets propagating the conspiracy theories with our assigned names, and Figure 1b shows the same data but scales each by the number of tweets that day (i.e., relative share of each conspiracy theory rather than total amount). First, note that the decline in conspiracy theory tweets around week 23 is driven by the George Floyd killing, which drew attention away from the coronavirus. Second, conspiracy theories were becoming active around week 12, and the conspiracy theory claiming that China created the virus was one of the first to show activity (Figure 1b). The following weeks show significant fluctuation in the number and proportion of tweets propagating each conspiracy theory. The aggregate fluctuations show an instability in propagation inconsistent with individuals holding stable beliefs in a specific conspiracy theory.

To further examine whether each individual stays faithful to one (or a few) conspiracy theories over time, Figure 2 shows the correlation coefficients of each pair of conspiracy theories, including a final column of bot likelihood. The figure clearly shows transitions between conspiracy theories, with nearly all correlation coefficients being positive, even though many of the conspiracy theories are inconsistent. Again, the evidence of instability contradicts a model of stable beliefs and suggests culture as a toolkit as an alternative model. The only column that shows significant negative correlations is bot likelihood, indicating that bot creators dislike certain conspiracy theories. Consistent with our intuition that bots favor bioweapon conspiracy theories, we find negative correlations with all the exaggerated threat conspiracies.

Figure 3 shows the diversity of conspiracy theories propagated by each individual by displaying the kernel density of the Gini inequality of conspiracy theories over their entire "career" as conspiracy theorists. The comparison of human users and bots reveals that humans have greater diversity of conspiracy theory propagation, consistent with the flexibility of the culture-as-a-toolkit models, whereas bots have higher consistency of conspiracy theories. Indeed, Figure 4 shows that human users propagated approximately more than two conspiracy theories, on average, in each month. By contrast, although bots were not quite stable, they were closer than humans to a model of stable beliefs.

Figure 5 evaluates whether users enter the world of conspiracy theories via more plausible (and thus less extreme) "gateway" conspiracies. Panel A in Figure 5 graphs the order of conspiracy theory adoption and highlights two conspiracy theories that display behavioral patterns consistent with gateways: false-positive testing and miscounted deaths. If conspiracy theory adoption were random, false-positive testing (and every other conspiracy) would be a user's first conspiracy approximately 8 percent of the time. Yet, falsepositive testing is first 27 percent of the time, and miscounted deaths is first 13 percent of the time. Consistent with the gateway hypothesis, these conspiracies are relatively minor, in that they acknowledge COVID-19 is real and dangerous but postulate that the threat is exaggerated. This stands in contrast to the frequency of more extreme bioweapon (Bill Gates; 7 percent) or hoax (empty hospitals; 5 percent) conspiracy theories. Humans are significantly more likely than bots to enter via a gateway (Figure 5, Panel B), supporting the notion that gateway conspiracy theories form a psychological bridge to more extreme beliefs.

These individual-level behavioral tendencies have aggregate consequences, as shown in Figure 6. This graph shows the aggregate transitions among conspiracy theories at three time points: February/March (left column), April/May (center), and June/July (right). Each conspiracy theory propagated by a user is counted as one, and the logarithm of the count forms the height of the bars. One individual propagating multiple conspiracy theories will appear multiple times (but multiple tweets of a single conspiracy theory will be counted as one). Similarly, the lines connecting bars are weighted by the number of transitions, and again one individual can make multiple transitions and hence appear in multiple lines. The graph shows significant traffic among compatible conspiracy theories, such as miscounted deaths and false positives, but also traffic between incompatible ones, such as China to false positives and miscounted deaths, or traffic centered on Bill Gates to and from nearly all other conspiracy theories. Again, the graph is suggestive of a population in which a high proportion of individuals are following the culture as a toolkit model.

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
paragraphs: Table 3 shows the analysis of the propagation of new conspiracy theories by users, as modeled via Poisson regression with exponentiated coefficients. Among the control variables in Model 1, we observe a positive first-order effect of prior conspiracy theory propagation, prior conspiracy count, indicating that once a user propagates one conspiracy theory, the likelihood they will propagate additional ones increases. The second-order term is negative, indicating that the magnitude of this effect diminishes as users exhaust the set of possible theories. Interestingly, we find that more well-connected individuals, as measured by log degree, are less likely to propagate new conspiracy theories. 3 Also of interest is the significantly positive effect of the maximum number of retweets a user receives, max retweets, on future conspiracy theory propagation. One might expect individuals to double-down on conspiracy theories that garnered them significant attention in the community, but we instead find that they diversify their propagation in response to high-impact tweets. Finally, we observe that bots are more likely to endorse new conspiracy theories, relative to human accounts. Turning to our primary independent variable of interest, case rate change, we find that an increase in the case rate positively predicts new conspiracy theory propagation (Model 1). This finding clearly shows the conspiracy theorist as a flexible and defensive person who propagates more conspiracy theories when the threat (here, infections) increases in severity. Model 2 interacts the case rate change with our indicator variable for whether an account is a bot and demonstrates that bots' conspiracy is significantly less susceptible to shifts in the external threat. This finding adds credence to our interpretation: the COVID-19 case rate measures mortality salience, which bots are less sensitive to because they do not need to make sense of and cope with threat. Model 3 interacts the max retweets variable with the bot variable, finding that bots do not differ from humans in their response to community engagement. Finally, Model 4 includes user fixed effects to examine within-user change over time. The coefficient for case rate change significantly increases in magnitude, strengthening the support for threat-driven conspiracy theory propagation. One interpretation for this finding is that users who are less inclined toward conspiratorial ideation are drawn into the sample when threat is high. Interaction terms from Models 2 and 3 are not significant.

Viewing conspiracy theories as elements of a toolkit does not just imply conspiracy acquisition and propagation, however, but alsomore fundamentally-questions whether the conspiracy theories one propagates need to be compatible. A toolkit view does not require incompatibility, but it does allow for it, and the limited number of compatible conspiracy theories available makes acquisition of incompatible ones difficult to avoid if one does not apply compatibility checks. Table 4 tests for individuals propagating incompatible conspiracy theories. It separates ever propagating incompatible theories, which could be due to conversion to a different view, and propagating incompatible theories during the same week, which shows concurrent inconsistency. Model 5 in Table 4 estimates the likelihood that a user propagates two contradictory conspiracies within the same week using a Weibull specification of the hazard model. We find a significant effect of an increase in the number of cases on propagating incompatible conspiracies during the same week. This result supports the toolkit view on conspiracy talk, as individuals are not only more likely to increase the number of conspiracies they propagate when threat increases, they are also more likely to propagate logically incompatible conspiracy theories. We do not observe any difference between bot and human accounts. Model 6 in Table 4 estimates whether individuals will propagate a conspiracy theory that is incompatible with any theory propagated earlier. People may begin believing the coronavirus is a hoax, but over time, as the number of deaths rise and friends and family become infected, they may revise their stance, believing the virus is orchestrated by a cabal of globalist elites. Here, we find a marginally significant negative effect of an increase in the case rate. This result suggests individuals are not revising their existing belief set. Instead, such contradictions occur jointly as individuals seek to make sense of their reality. We find that bots are more likely than human accounts to cycle through conspiracies in this manner. Thus, the best fit to the data on both new conspiracy theory propagation and incompatible conspiracy theory propagation by humans is provided by viewing conspiracy theories as a toolkit.

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
paragraphs: In a review of rumor research, Rosnow (1991:491) noted that "the data base continues to be more wanting than daunting and the balance between data and theory is still tilted decidedly in favor of the latter." Our research described conspiracy talk on Twitter during the coronavirus pandemic and, in contrast to prior work, shifted attention from self-reports of individual beliefs as gathered through surveys to revealed behaviors-whether individuals retweeted a single version of the conspiracy or multiple versions. Prior surveybased work, like Uscinski and colleagues (2020) who conducted a survey and categorized individuals into whether they believe COVID-19 is a hoax or a bioweapon spread by powerful individuals or impersonal cabals, strips away the social context of conspiratorial talk in online platforms.

Our study complements Bail (2021) and Munger and Phillips (2022) by documenting the cognitive architecture of COVID-19 conspiracy theories and illuminating how social interactions, rather than algorithms, shape the trajectory of online conspiracy talk. Instead of individuals being directed by algorithms into rabbit holes of radicalism, a key descriptive finding is that people enter online conspiracy talk via a gateway conspiracy-an easy-tobelieve and plausible account. In our case, the false-positive testing and miscounted deaths theories both acknowledged that COVID-19 is real but downplayed its threat and were the most plausible of the conspiracy theories. A second descriptive finding is that people diversify their conspiracy talk: they embrace COVID-19 talk that implies the virus is an exaggerated hoax, as well as talk that characterizes COVID-19 as spread on purpose, a bioweapon unleashed by malignant actors.

Our research described conspiracy talk on Twitter during the coronavirus pandemic and, in contrast to prior work, shifted attention from self-reports of individual beliefs as gathered through surveys to revealed behaviors-whether individuals retweeted a single version of the conspiracy or multiple versions. Prior surveybased work, like Uscinski and colleagues (2020) who conducted a survey and categorized individuals into whether they believe COVID-19 is a hoax or a bioweapon spread by powerful individuals or impersonal cabals, strips away the social context of conspiratorial talk in online platforms.
sections.preview_tail: Instead of individuals being directed by algorithms into rabbit holes of radicalism, a key descriptive finding is that people enter online conspiracy talk via a gateway conspiracy-an easy-tobelieve and plausible account. In our case, the false-positive testing and miscounted deaths theories both acknowledged that COVID-19 is real but downplayed its threat and were the most plausible of the conspiracy theories. A second descriptive finding is that people diversify their conspiracy talk: they embrace COVID-19 talk that implies the virus is an exaggerated hoax, as well as talk that characterizes COVID-19 as spread on purpose, a bioweapon unleashed by malignant actors.

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
sections.header_raw: People as Bricoleurs in Conspiracy Talk
sections.header_norm: people as bricoleurs in conspiracy talk
paragraphs: These results challenge traditional views of conspiracy talk as being located within specific individuals, such as the "authoritarian personality," or in small and closed social circles, such as cults or echo chambers. Our study indicates that participants in such talk were not the paranoid individuals imagined by Adorno, but instead ordinary people seeking to create and sustain meaning, as outlined by Lowenthal and Guterman (1949), and engaged in sensemaking, as discussed by Weick (1995). In the open forum of Twitter, conspiracy talk was not the product of closed social circles of like-minded people, similar to a cult, where a charismatic leader is the source of homogenous thought and cult members promote it. Instead of uniformity of explanations, there was diversity of explanations. Our results show that individuals adopt new conspiracy theories and inconsistent logics as threat intensifies from a rising COVID-19 case rate, after they garner attention and peer approval in the form of retweets.

A practical implication is that people are not engineers working according to a blueprint, but are instead bricoleurs (Levi-Strauss 1996). An important component of a bricoleur's toolkit is flexibility, which is especially valuable when facing threats. An individual using a conspiracy theory as a shield against reality may find friends and acquaintances challenging the conspiracy theory, leading to the possibility of facing social resistance and even having to accept unpleasant facts. Multiple conspiracy theories are better than a single one, because they can be used more flexibly and can protect the denial of reality. For reality denial, conspiracy theories are superior to actual explanations, and multiple conspiracy theories are superior to one. Hence, individuals engaged in reality denial should have a greater likelihood of adopting additional conspiracy theories after adopting the first one. These conspiracy theories do not need to be compatible with each other because they are used selectively depending on circumstances, and hence will not be tested for compatibility in any given interaction. Indeed, Olmsted (2009) points out that Americans treat conspiracy theories as if they were true, rather than fully believing in them.

The potential flexibility in propagating multiple conspiracy theories stems from a view of culture as a toolkit, comprising conflicting symbols, explanations, and myths (Swidler 1986:277). Swidler (1986:278-79) distinguishes between settled times and unsettled times and observes that in unsettled times "people formulate, flesh out new lines of action." Supporting this notion, new conspiracy theories flourish following major societal events, as individuals develop new ways to interpret and make sense of new situations (Uscinski and Parent 2014). This perspective allows for people propagating incompatible conspiratorial versions of the same event simultaneously.
