sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
sections.header_raw: Bots, Conspiracy Talk, and Moral Panic
sections.header_norm: bots conspiracy talk and moral panic
paragraphs: Our study also distinguished between tweets from bots and tweets from human beings using Botometer, a machine-learning classifier for Twitter accounts (Davis et al. 2016). Bots are more active on Twitter and more likely to propagate conspiracy theories. In our study, bots differed from people in nearly every way, and in ways that heighten the salience of the cultural toolkit model. Bots were impervious to increasing COVID-19 cases, thereby suggesting their controllers' motivations had little to do with reducing threat. Bots did not require a gateway conspiracy, and they promoted more extreme versions of the COVID-19 conspiracy. Bots tended to be more specialized and to propagate conspiracy theories that implied COVID-19 was a bioweapon. Bots were inconsistent, just as people were, but they took longer to change, suggesting they were likely switching to latch on to new conspiracy theories that had better traction. Ironically, an observer of bot behavior, who believed the bots were human, might conclude from their persistent and extreme beliefs that they were cult members.

In short, bot designers seek to sow distrust and propagate moral panics. Cohen (1972:9) observed that "societies appear to be subject, every now and then, to periods of moral panic. A condition, episode, person or group of persons emerges to become defined as a threat to societal values and interests." For example, Puritan religious leaders fostered hysteria about the witches in Salem, and launched trials to denounce them, to preserve the moral boundaries of the Puritan community. The moral panic literature suggests that actors who create moral panics may be elites, general interest groups, or grassroots movements. However, the role of social media in amplifying moral panics has been neglected (Walsh 2020), and the role of bots as purveyors of moral panic has languished. In this sense, our results with respect to bots may be seen as a corrective to the literature.

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
sections.header_raw: Offense and Defense in Conspiracy Talk
sections.header_norm: offense and defense in conspiracy talk
paragraphs: The conspiracy talk by people was defensive in nature. As Clarke (2002Clarke ( , 2007) ) points out, conspiracy talk is a degenerating research program in the sense developed by Lakatos (1970). In a degenerating research program, "successful novel predictions and retrodictions are not made. Instead, auxiliary hypotheses and initial conditions are successively modified in light of new evidence, to protect the original theory from apparent disconfirmation" (Clarke 2002:136). Lakatos (1970) defined a research program as consisting of a "hard core" of unquestioned assumptions, and a "protective belt" of auxiliary hypotheses designed to shield the hard core. In a progressive research program, participants develop novel predictions and even ideas that may disconfirm the hard core. In degenerate research programs, the emphasis is to protect the hard core from disconfirmation. Under the societal conditions present during our study, conspiracy talk was widespread in an open community of participants in a platform (Twitter) in which anyone could observe and respond to conspiracy theory statements, seeking to overturn their assumptions and falsify their claims. Indeed, the lack of verification allows for additional versions to be generated to protect the hard core-a distrust of authority. To each participant in conspiracy talk, it does not matter if a single conspiracy theory faces resistance and persuasive rejection; there are many more to choose from.

It would be possible to maintain multiple conspiracy theories and ensure they are compatible with each other, so that when confronted with detailed information that falsifies one element of a conspiracy theory, this theory can be abandoned and another in the same family can be adopted. For example, an individual who is persuaded that it is not possible for Bill Gates to implant people with microchip tracking devices through vaccines can switch to the belief that research initiated by Dr. Anthony Fauci produced and released the virus. Such consistency comes at the cost of being vulnerable to more forms of opposition and falsification, compared to holding a host of conspiracy theories with no consistency checks. Just as a degenerating research program can have incompatible statements from ad hoc modifications to counter respective disconfirmation, a conspiracy theorist can hold multiple incompatible theories. Visually, conspiracy talk is "diverse nodes correlated and fitted together in unusual fashion. Like hyper-links stretching out on the World Wide Web, conspiracy narratives are hyper-narratives, weird and wonderfully interconnected spiders' web plots" (Skinner 2001:94).

It is perhaps surprising that conspiracy talk could be defensive in nature when some of the conspiracy theories had a logic of action describing the virus as a bioweapon deployed to serve the purpose of powerful actors (e.g., Bill Gates, China). It is important to keep in mind that these conspiracy theories are sensemaking devices used by individuals who do not believe the press and the authorities providing the news, such as the state or the medical establishment. If an individual does not believe the official story that COVID-19 is a virus that mindlessly and mechanically attaches itself to people and uses them to reproduce itself, the possibility that many people get sick and die is disturbing and threatening. The ability to make sense of it, even if it involves stories about mythical overlords, may provide relief.

sections.paper_id: Greve et al. - 2022 - Online Conspiracy Groups Micro-Bloggers, Bots, and Coronavirus Conspiracy Talk on Twitter
paragraphs: The limitations of our study point to promising directions for future research. To our knowledge, the study of real-time online conspiracy theory talk in sociology is scarce, so our goals were descriptive. Future research needs to pin down the causal antecedents of multiple conspiracy theories and people's choices of which to propagate and when to propagate additional ones. Future work could also shed light on the sequence of transitions among conspiracy theories with different degrees of plausibility and different targets. Because there are many possible sequences, machine-learning techniques will be helpful in predicting the sequences.

Our study discussed conspiracy talkexplanations or justifications for the coronavirus-but did not study whether such talk had motivational consequences. Although it is natural to assume that propagation of multiple conspiracy theories is correlated with opposition to vaccines, isolation, and masking, this assumption should be further tested (see Bierwiaczonek et al. 2020). Moreover, if retweeting a conspiracy explanation is indicative of distrust in public health and government authorities, does such distrust manifest in more extreme forms of voice against public health initiatives or threats against schoolboard members and other representatives of officialdom? Although our study suggests conspiracy talk may have deadly consequences, additional research is needed to establish any forward link to mask rejection and vaccine reluctance-and even to violence.

A key implication is that traditional means of persuasion, marketing, and public relations will be ineffective against conspiracy talk because they make an incorrect assumption about individuals. The cognitive process does not start with an incorrect view of the world in the form of a conspiracy theory and end in vaccination rejection. It starts with denial of a world with a deadly pandemic and ends with propagation of conspiracy theories that allows continued denials. Rejecting the content of conspiracy theories treats the symptom, not the illness, and is unlikely to be effective. These considerations lead to the key question that emerged from our work. How does the effective online propagation of conspiracy theories discovered here affect the presence and acceptance of reality denial in society?

Our research highlights one potential area to target for intervention: gateway conspiracies. The descent into reality denial begins with a first step; on Twitter, users began with plausible theories of inaccurate testing and death statistics. The apparent innocuousness of gateway conspiracy theories, particularly in relation to their extreme counterparts, belies their importance. Corrective messaging attacking gateway conspiracies offers a promising pathway to combat misinformation and conspiracy theories, because individuals have yet to fully detach from reality and may be more receptive to these interventions.

sections.preview_head: The limitations of our study point to promising directions for future research. To our knowledge, the study of real-time online conspiracy theory talk in sociology is scarce, so our goals were descriptive. Future research needs to pin down the causal antecedents of multiple conspiracy theories and people's choices of which to propagate and when to propagate additional ones.
sections.preview_tail: The descent into reality denial begins with a first step; on Twitter, users began with plausible theories of inaccurate testing and death statistics. The apparent innocuousness of gateway conspiracy theories, particularly in relation to their extreme counterparts, belies their importance. Corrective messaging attacking gateway conspiracies offers a promising pathway to combat misinformation and conspiracy theories, because individuals have yet to fully detach from reality and may be more receptive to these interventions.
